ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for raisin bun; closest defensible anchor is bread roll (FOODON_03302050). Raisin bun is a sweet enriched bread roll with dried raisins.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and rolls as a category; no specific raisin bun sub-code exists. Assigned at category level as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raisin bun as a specific food concept; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard raisin bun weighing approximately 75 g, as commonly sold in Singapore bakeries and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Raisin Bun — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Raisin Bun (cr_id F05020100). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Bread, raisin, enriched' (FDC ID 325871) as an adapted secondary reference.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for white bread with raisins / enriched raisin bread
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a Singapore-style raisin bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 71 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: raisin bread / enriched white bread with raisins (GI ~71, glucose reference, University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (71) × available carbohydrate per serving (49.1 g/100g × 75 g serving = 36.8 g) ÷ 100 = 26.1, rounded to 26.0. This is a high-GI, high-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 185 mg/100g and high sugar 14.6 g/100g with moderate refined carbohydrate load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit raisin bun consumption to one small bun occasionally, as the sodium content (185 mg/100g) contributes meaningfully to daily sodium targets and the high sugar load (14.6 g/100g) may promote insulin-driven sodium retention; plain wholemeal bread is a lower-sodium, lower-sugar alternative for regular use.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~71), high sugar 14.6 g/100g, high carbohydrate 49.1 g/100g, and low dietary fibre 2.7 g/100g from refined wheat flour
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit raisin buns due to a high glycemic index (~71) and glycemic load (~26 per bun), driven by refined wheat flour and concentrated raisin sugars (14.6 g/100g), which cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, limit to half a bun and pair with a protein source to partially attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar 14.6 g/100g and refined carbohydrate with low fibre, contributing to dyslipidaemia and elevated triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, raisin buns should be consumed sparingly; the high sugar content (14.6 g/100g) and refined carbohydrate load (49.1 g/100g with only 2.7 g/100g fibre) can raise triglycerides and contribute to atherogenic dyslipidaemia, while the saturated fat from butter (~0.9 g/100g) adds a modest additional cardiovascular risk; wholegrain alternatives with lower sugar are preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar 14.6 g/100g and high refined carbohydrate glycaemic load promoting pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis should avoid raisin buns regularly, as the high sugar (14.6 g/100g) and refined carbohydrate load (49.1 g/100g, fibre only 2.7 g/100g) drive a high glycaemic load that promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients are present to offset this effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A raisin bun is a soft, sweet, enriched bread roll made from refined wheat flour, sugar, butter, eggs, yeast, and dried raisins distributed throughout the dough. It is baked until golden and has a tender, slightly chewy crumb with a mildly sweet flavour and occasional bursts of sweetness from the plump raisins. The crust is soft and lightly glazed in many commercial versions. Raisin buns are widely consumed in Singapore and across Western-influenced bakery traditions as a breakfast item or snack. Per 100 g, the bun provides 268 kcal, 8.1 g protein, 3.8 g fat, 49.1 g carbohydrate, 14.6 g sugar, 2.7 g dietary fibre, and 185 mg sodium. The high sugar content (14.6 g/100g) and refined carbohydrate profile give this food a high glycemic index (~71) and a high glycemic load (~26 per 75 g bun), making it clinically significant for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. It is not suitable for regular consumption by people managing blood glucose or inflammatory conditions. Calcium (44 mg/100g) and iron (2.1 mg/100g) are present at modest levels from the enriched flour and raisins.

Is raisin bun high in sugar?

Raisin bun vs plain bread calories

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020100, confidence 0.95) which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for enriched raisin bread as a secondary approved source. GI estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database (raisin bread, GI ~71) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.9 g/100g) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Bread, raisin, enriched' (FDC ID 325871) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry and are not available from the adapted USDA SR Legacy reference at the required specificity for a Singapore-style raisin bun. 3. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (raisin bread / enriched white bread with raisins); no direct GI measurement for a Singapore-style raisin bun was found in approved sources. 4. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a Western-origin bakery product widely sold in Singapore. Multilingual naming rules requiring Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi apply only to Singaporean dishes; this product does not qualify under that rule and local_names fields are appropriately left null. 5. No zero-value core macros are present; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
